content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 14, 2019


Charley Hesse
California Department of Parks & Recreation
Division of Boating and Waterways
One Capitol Mall, Suite 500
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-19-219

Dear Mr. Hesse:

       This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions
such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

       Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 7), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

       Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions restrict your ability to obtain a permit to operate
your business at a state park facility, given that you are also an employee of the Department of
Parks & Recreation?

CONCLUSION

       No. Where you will be obtaining a permit on the same terms and conditions as any other
member of the general public, from Associated Students, Inc., to operate your business on park
property, the conflict of interest provisions of Act would not be implicated.


_______________
       ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-219
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are a State Parks employee and work fulltime as an Associate Government Program Analyst. You act as a Commission Liaison, Regulation Analyst, and a Project Manager. You state that you do not make governmental decisions in this capacity, as you are not a manager.

You also own a business, which you operate on weekends. You plan to attend a regatta event in May at the Sacramento Sate Aquatic Center, where your business would sell t-shirts. The Sacramento Sate Aquatic Center is located at Lake Natoma, and is a joint project between California State Parks and CSUS. You will need to obtain a vendor permit to sell at this event, which involves paying a vendor fee. The fee is payable to Associated Students, Inc., the entity that operates the aquatic center, and would be available to you on the same terms and conditions as any other vendor. As this event is being held within the boundary of a state park site, the Superintendent that is in charge of the park indicated that you need a signed statement declaring that you do not have a conflict of interest.

ANALYSIS

Making, Participating in, or Influencing a Decision

Under Section 87100, "[n]o public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest." An official "makes" a government decision if the official "authorizes or directs any action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his agency." (Regulation 18704(a).) "Participating in" a government decision occurs when the official "provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the purpose of affecting the decision without significant intervening substantive review." (Regulation 18704(b).) Lastly, a public official "uses his or her position to influence a decision" if the official:

(1) Contacts or appears before any official in his or her agency or in an agency subject to the authority or budgetary control of his or her agency for the purpose of affecting a decision; or

(2) Contacts or appears before any official in any other government agency for the purpose of affecting a decision, and the public official acts or purports to act within his or her authority or on behalf of his or her agency in making the contact.

(Regulation 18704(c).)

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to disqualify a public official from "making," "participating in making," or "influencing" a governmental decision. Based on the facts presented, you are not making, participation in making, or using your position to influence a governmental decision by paying for and obtaining a vendor permit from Associated Students, Inc. to operate your business at the Sacramento Sate Aquatic Center as a member of the general public, and the conflict of interest provisions of Act would not be implicated. We caution, however, that

File No. A-19-219
Page No. 3

this conclusion is limited to you obtaining the permit on the same terms and conditions as any other
member of the general public and you may not purport to act in your official governmental capacity
or on behalf of your agency in obtaining the permit. You may also wish to speak to your agency's
counsel regarding any restrictions on incompatible activities your agency may impose. The
Commission can provide advice only regarding the conflicts of interest provisions of the Act.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:sal
